transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Patrick. I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been feeling a lot of pressure at work lately. There have been some issues with spreadsheets, and it feels like I'm being blamed for things that aren't really my fault. It’s just frustrating, you know? I don’t know if I’m handling it the right way.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're experiencing a lot of frustration from feeling pressured at work, especially with the issues surrounding the spreadsheets. It must be tough to feel blamed for things that you believe aren’t your fault. Can you share a bit more about how this pressure is affecting you emotionally?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s definitely affecting me. I feel anxious and worried all the time, like I’m constantly on edge. There’s this fear that I’m not doing enough, or that I’m somehow weak for not being able to handle it better. It’s like I’m stuck in this cycle of feeling scared and angry at the same time. I just want to explain what really happened, but it feels like no one is listening.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really overwhelming to be caught in that cycle of anxiety and anger. Feeling like you’re not being heard can add to that frustration, especially when you want to clarify your side of things. It’s understandable to feel scared and worried in that situation. Can you tell me more about what you think might be contributing to that feeling of not doing enough?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess it’s just this constant pressure to fix things, even when I know I didn’t cause the problems. I feel like everyone expects me to have all the answers, and when things go wrong, it’s like I’m the one who gets pointed at. It makes me question my abilities, like maybe I really am weak or not cut out for this. I don’t want to let anyone down, but it’s hard when I feel like I’m being scapegoated. It’s just... exhausting.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That does sound exhausting, Patrick. It seems like the pressure to have all the answers and the fear of letting others down is weighing heavily on you. It’s understandable to question your abilities when you feel scapegoated like that. I hear you saying that you’re feeling both the burden of expectations and the frustration of being unfairly blamed. How do you usually cope with these feelings when they arise?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I try to explain the situation to my boss, hoping that maybe they’ll understand where I’m coming from. But honestly, it often feels like it doesn’t make a difference. I end up feeling frustrated and discouraged, like I’m just going in circles. Sometimes I just try to push through it, but it’s hard to shake off that feeling of being powerless. I guess I’m still figuring out how to deal with it all.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re putting in a lot of effort to communicate and seek understanding, but it’s disheartening when it feels like it doesn’t lead to any real change. Pushing through can be a tough coping mechanism, especially when you’re feeling powerless. It’s okay to still be figuring things out. What do you think would help you feel more empowered in this situation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it would help if I could find a way to communicate more effectively, maybe even get some support from my colleagues. Sometimes I wonder if I’m just not expressing myself clearly enough. If I could feel more confident in standing up for myself and my work, that might make a difference. But it’s hard to shake off that feeling of being overwhelmed and unsure. I guess I just want to feel like I have some control over the situation.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes sense that you want to feel more confident in expressing yourself and seeking support from your colleagues. Feeling in control can really change how you experience those pressures. It’s understandable to feel overwhelmed when you’re trying to navigate all of this. What do you think might be the first step toward finding that clarity in your communication or building that support with your colleagues?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe I could start by having a more open conversation with a couple of my colleagues who I trust. Just sharing what I’m experiencing might help me feel less alone in this. I could also try to clarify my role in these situations, so everyone knows what to expect from me. It’s just... taking that first step feels daunting. I guess I worry about how they’ll react or if they’ll think I’m overreacting. But I know I need to try something different.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like reaching out to trusted colleagues and clarifying your role could be a positive step forward for you. It’s completely natural to feel daunted by that first step, especially with the fear of how they might react. It shows a lot of insight that you recognize the need for a different approach. What do you think might help ease that fear as you prepare to have those conversations?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it might help to remind myself that everyone makes mistakes and that it’s okay to ask for support. Maybe I could practice what I want to say beforehand, so I feel a bit more prepared. It could also be reassuring to remember that I’m not alone in this—others might be feeling similar pressures. Just having a plan in place could make it feel less overwhelming. I guess I just need to take it one step at a time.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful approach, Patrick. Reminding yourself that it’s okay to ask for support and that everyone makes mistakes can really help shift your perspective. Practicing what you want to say and having a plan in place can also give you a sense of control. Taking it one step at a time is a wise way to navigate through those feelings of overwhelm. How do you feel about setting a timeline for when you might start having those conversations with your colleagues?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Setting a timeline could be helpful, actually. Maybe I could aim to have a conversation within the next week or so. That gives me a little time to prepare and gather my thoughts. I think if I set a specific day, it might motivate me to actually follow through instead of just putting it off. I just hope I can stay calm and focused when the time comes. It’s a bit nerve-wracking, but I know it’s a step I need to take.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a solid plan, Patrick. Aiming for a specific day within the next week gives you a clear goal to work toward, and it’s great that you’re recognizing the importance of preparation. It’s completely normal to feel nervous about it, but it also shows that you care about how you communicate. As the day approaches, what do you think might help you stay calm and focused during the conversation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it might help to take some deep breaths before the conversation, just to calm my nerves a bit. Maybe I could also remind myself that it’s a conversation, not an interrogation. Keeping it more casual might help ease the pressure. I could focus on listening as much as talking, too, so it feels more like a dialogue. Just having that mindset might help me stay grounded and less anxious.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are excellent strategies, Patrick. Taking deep breaths can really help center you, and approaching it as a dialogue rather than an interrogation can shift the dynamic to something more comfortable. Focusing on listening as well as sharing can foster a more collaborative atmosphere. It sounds like you’re developing a thoughtful mindset that could really support you in this conversation. How do you feel about the progress you’ve made in our discussion today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit more hopeful, actually. Talking through everything has helped me see some potential steps I can take, and it feels good to have a plan in mind. I still have some nerves about the conversations ahead, but I think I’m starting to feel a little more empowered. It’s nice to know that I can reach out for support and that it’s okay to express what I’m going through. I appreciate having this space to talk about it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that you’re feeling more hopeful and empowered, Patrick. It’s great that you’ve identified potential steps and recognized the value of reaching out for support. Having a space to express what you’re going through can make a big difference. As you move forward with your plan, remember that it’s okay to take things at your own pace. If you ever want to talk more about how those conversations go or anything else that comes up, I’m here for you. What’s one thing you’d like to take away from our conversation today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think one key takeaway for me is the importance of reaching out and communicating with my colleagues. It’s easy to feel isolated in my struggles, but knowing that I can share my experiences and ask for support feels like a big step. I also want to remember to be kind to myself during this process and not to put too much pressure on myself. Just taking that first step is what matters. Thank you for helping me see that.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a wonderful takeaway, Patrick. Recognizing the importance of reaching out and being kind to yourself is so valuable, especially during challenging times. It’s all about those first steps and allowing yourself the space to navigate through it. I’m glad I could support you in seeing that. If you ever want to revisit these themes or explore anything else, feel free to reach out. Wishing you the best as you take those next steps!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I really appreciate that. It means a lot to have this support and to know that I can come back to talk about anything that comes up. I’ll definitely keep those takeaways in mind as I move forward. Thanks again for your understanding and guidance today.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Patrick. I’m glad to have been here for you today, and it’s great to hear that you feel supported. Remember, it’s perfectly okay to reach out whenever you need to talk or explore anything further. Take care as you move forward, and I look forward to hearing how things go for you.

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I’ll definitely keep that in mind. I appreciate your support and understanding. I’ll take care and hopefully have some positive updates next time we talk. Take care, and see you soon!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re welcome, Patrick! I’m looking forward to hearing your updates and supporting you along the way. Take care of yourself, and see you soon!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I will. Take care, and see you soon!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Take care, Patrick! Looking forward to our next conversation. See you soon!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: See you soon! Take care!
